The hands-on labs were the most useful part — I learned more in two hours of debugging with a TA than I did reading the textbook.
Recursion was hard at first but the 'leap of faith' framing finally made it click around week 6.
More worked examples on the slides would help. I understand the concept in lecture but blank when I see a new problem.
Office hours were a lifesaver. The TAs are patient and don't make me feel dumb for asking basic questions.
The autograder feedback is usually helpful but sometimes the error messages are cryptic — would love clearer hints when a test fails.
ArrayList is the most useful thing I learned this semester. I keep finding uses for it outside class.
Lab pacing is rough. I either finish in 30 minutes or run out of time completely.
Loved the meme channel honestly, made the class feel less intimidating.
Pace was a bit slow in the first half, then jumped sharply with recursion.
Could the homework specs be split into checkpoints? Right now it's one giant doc and I miss requirements.
I came in with zero experience and now I feel comfortable writing small Java programs. Big confidence boost.
The midterm was harder than the practice exams suggested.
Inheritance examples could be more practical — I get the concept but the shape hierarchy feels contrived.
Late tokens are a great policy. Took the stress off when my laptop broke.
I wish there were more group projects. Working alone all semester felt isolating.
GitHub setup at the start of the semester was painful. Took me two weeks to feel comfortable pushing code.
I struggled with whether I belong in this major all semester but the staff really helped me stick with it.
Honestly, more lectures and fewer assignments would be my preference.
The grading rubric is clear and I always know why I lost points. Appreciate that.
The discussion forum is where I learned the most — seeing how other people got stuck helped me unstuck myself.
Could you put the slides up before lecture instead of after? Hard to take notes otherwise.
Best CS class I've taken. Would recommend even to non-majors.
I'd like more focus on debugging skills. Reading a stack trace is its own art form.
Surveys like this one are taking up time I'd rather spend on the assignment, just FYI.
Loops are still confusing for me even after a whole semester. Maybe more drill exercises?
OOP made way more sense after the shapes lab. I needed something concrete.
The professor's energy in lecture genuinely makes me want to come.
Practice midterms should be available earlier — I only had two days with them.
Sometimes the autograder gives me 0 even when my code runs locally. That's frustrating.
I learned Java AND how to use a debugger this semester. Both feel like superpowers.
